channel.title: Transformer on Yiyue Liu's Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Transformer on Yiyue Liu's Blog

item.title: Transformer: Advanced Topics
item.description:
This post continues the Transformer series and focuses on the changes that made the original architecture work better for large language models. The core idea of attention is still the same, but several parts of the 2017 design are now commonly replaced:

  • Decoder-Only Instead of Encoder-Decoder
  • Pre-Norm Instead of Post-Norm
  • RMSNorm Instead of LayerNorm
  • RoPE Instead of Absolute Positional Encoding
  • GQA, MQA, and MLA Instead of MHA
  • GLU instead of GELU in FFN/MLP

As a refresher, the below is the original transformer architecture:

item.title: Transformer: Parameter Count, FLOPs, Memory Usage, and Training Time
item.description:
This post continues the Transformer article and focuses on model size and compute estimation.

Terminology #

$b$ is batch size, $s$ is sequence length, $h$ is hidden dimension, $a$ is the number of attention heads, and $d_{\mathrm{head}}$ is head dimension.

  • hidden_dim: the last dimension in [batch, sequence, hidden_dim]. It is also called hidden size, model dimension, embedding dimension, d_model, hidden_size, or n_embd.

  • head_dim: the dimension of each attention head, also written as d_head or head_size. Usually, head_dim = hidden_dim / num_heads. After Q/K/V projection, Q is [batch, seq_len, num_heads, head_dim], K is [batch, seq_len, num_heads, head_dim], and V is [batch, seq_len, num_heads, head_dim]

item.title: Transformer: Basics
item.description:
Table of Contents #

  • Overview
  • Encoder-Only, Decoder-Only, and Encoder-Decoder Tasks
  • Transformer vs. RNN
  • Input Processing and Embeddings
  • Positional Encoding
  • Attention and Its Math*
  • MLP or FFN
  • Residual Connection
  • Batch Norm and Layer Norm
  • Complete PyTorch Code

Overview #

Transformer is a neural network architecture designed to process sequences. It was introduced as an alternative to RNN-based models, with the core idea that a model should not pass information only step by step through time. Instead, it should be able to directly look at the whole sequence and decide which parts matter most.
